diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: One limb of the FAST triad; combined with limb and speech signs it is highly specific for stroke.
Facial droop alone can occur in Bell's palsy, but not with the accompanying limb weakness and aphasia.

symptoms.symptom_name: Sudden right-sided arm and leg weakness (hemiparesis)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Contralateral hemiparesis localises to the motor cortex/tract; with aphasia it points to the dominant hemisphere MCA territory.
Limb weakness is never a feature of a peripheral facial nerve palsy — its presence excludes Bell's palsy.
Expressive aphasia localises language cortex involvement to the dominant (usually left) hemisphere MCA territory.
Abrupt, maximal-at-onset focal deficit is the hallmark of a vascular event.
Seizure-related deficits usually follow a convulsion and then improve; sudden fixed deficit favours stroke.
AF is a leading cause of cardioembolic stroke; unprotected AF is a strong mechanistic link to this event.

symptoms.symptom_name: Facial droop sparing the forehead (upper-motor-neuron pattern)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Forehead sparing indicates an upper-motor-neuron facial pattern (central), distinguishing stroke from a peripheral Bell's palsy.
Bell's palsy affects the whole hemiface including the forehead; sparing argues against it. This is a clinician-examined sign, sealed from telehealth.
Confirms atrial fibrillation as the embolic source; a palpated/auscultated clinician sign not obtainable over chat.

investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Hypoglycaemia, a rapidly reversible stroke mimic, would not be excluded.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A normal glucose excludes hypoglycaemia as the cause of the deficit.
Gaze deviation toward the side of the lesion indicates a large hemispheric infarct; a clinician-examined sign, sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: CT/MRI head showing infarction and excluding haemorrhage

investigation_details.expected_result: Acute infarct / restricted diffusion; no haemorrhage
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Ischaemic vs haemorrhagic stroke cannot be separated and reperfusion cannot be safely given.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Imaging is the definitive discriminator; never available in real time via telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Preserved consciousness, follows simple one-step commands

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Preserved comprehension does not lessen severity; expressive aphasia with dense hemiparesis is still a major stroke.

symptoms.symptom_name: No headache, no head trauma, no witnessed convulsion

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: No witnessed convulsion makes a post-ictal Todd's paresis less likely.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: acute ischaemic stroke, left MCA territory
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Sudden face + arm + speech deficits together are highly specific for acute stroke and mandate immediate emergency escalation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Excludes haemorrhage before any antithrombotic and identifies reperfusion candidacy — the first hospital step.
Excludes hypoglycaemia, the reversible mimic, and must be checked in every suspected stroke.
Confirms atrial fibrillation as the embolic source and screens for concurrent cardiac ischaemia.
Identifies large-vessel occlusion and salvageable penumbra for thrombectomy eligibility.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Point-of-care neuro-observation at home beyond FAST
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: The AI Doctor should not delay 000 to attempt detailed home neurological testing — recognition of FAST positivity is sufficient to escalate.
